text: The LORD's anger burned against Israel again, and he moved David to act against them, saying, go and count Israel and Judah.
So the king told Joab and the army commanders with him to go through all the tribes of Israel, from Dan to Beersheba, and register the fighting men, so he would know their number.
Joab answered the king that he hoped the LORD would multiply the troops a hundred times over, and that the king would live to see it, but asked why his lord the king wanted to do this at all.
But the king's word overruled Joab and the commanders, so they left the king's presence to register the fighting men of Israel.
They crossed the Jordan and camped near Aroer, south of the town in the valley, then went toward Gad and on to Jazer.
They went on to Gilead and the land of the Hittites toward Kadesh, then to Dan-jaan and around toward Sidon.
They came to the fortress of Tyre and all the towns of the Hivites and Canaanites, and finished at the Negev of Judah, at Beersheba.
After traveling through the whole land, they returned to Jerusalem at the end of nine months and twenty days.
Joab reported the total count of fighting men to the king: eight hundred thousand able swordsmen in Israel, and five hundred thousand in Judah.

Afterward, David's conscience struck him for having counted the troops, and he said to the LORD that he had sinned greatly in what he had done, and asked the LORD to take away his servant's guilt, for he had acted very foolishly.
When David got up the next morning, the word of the LORD had come to Gad, David's seer, saying,
go and tell David: this is what the LORD says — I am offering you three choices; pick one, and I will carry it out against you.

So Gad went to David and told him: shall seven years of famine come to your land, or three months of fleeing before your enemies while they chase you, or three days of plague in your land? Consider carefully what answer I should bring back to the one who sent me.
David told Gad he was in deep distress, but said, let us fall into the LORD's hand, for his mercies are great; do not let me fall into the hand of man.
So the LORD sent a plague on Israel from that morning until the appointed time, and seventy thousand people died, from Dan to Beersheba.

When the angel stretched out his hand toward Jerusalem to destroy it, the LORD relented over the disaster and told the angel who was striking the people, enough now, withdraw your hand — the angel was then beside the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite.

When David saw the angel striking the people, he said to the LORD, it is I who sinned, I who did wrong; but these are only sheep — what have they done? Let your hand fall against me and my father's family instead.
Gad came to David that day and told him to go up and set up an altar to the LORD on the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite.
So David went up as Gad had instructed, in keeping with the LORD's own command.
When Araunah looked out and saw the king and his officials coming toward him, he went out and bowed before the king with his face to the ground.
Araunah asked why his lord the king had come to his servant, and David answered that he had come to buy the threshing floor to build an altar to the LORD, so the plague on the people would stop.
Araunah told David to take whatever seemed good to him and offer it up: here were oxen for the burnt offering, and the threshing sledges and the yokes for wood.
Araunah gave it all to the king, everything, and added, may the LORD your God accept you.

But the king told Araunah no — he insisted on paying the full price, saying he would not offer burnt offerings to the LORD his God that cost him nothing; so David bought the threshing floor and the oxen for fifty shekels of silver.
David built an altar to the LORD there and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings; the LORD answered his prayer for the land, and the plague on Israel was stopped.
